attributes.stem:
This question is considered difficult even at the graduating medical student level.

A 42-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with three months of progressive exertional breathlessness and dry cough. She also reports joint pain, muscle weakness when climbing stairs, and cracking of the skin on her hands. She works as a hairdresser and has no significant medical history. She does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears short of breath at rest. Her pulse rate is 95/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. There are fine crackles at both lung bases. She has symmetrical proximal muscle weakness in her upper and lower limbs (grade 4/5). Her hands show hyperkeratotic, fissured lesions along the radial and palmar aspects. Her creatine kinase is 850 U/L (24-170).

Pulmonary function tests show: FEV1 65% predicted, FVC 62% predicted, FEV1/FVC ratio 85%, DLCO 45% predicted. High resolution CT chest demonstrates bilateral ground glass changes with reticular opacities in a predominantly peripheral and basal distribution.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations is most likely to assist in confirming the diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The constellation of symptoms in this patient is overall concerning for a connective tissue disease associated with interstitial lung disease (CT-ILD). More specifically this patient presents with features suggestive of anti-synthetase syndrome, which is characterised by the triad of inflammatory myositis, interstitial lung disease, and arthritis. Additional features include mechanic's hands (hyperkeratotic, fissured lesions), fever, and Raynaud's phenomenon. Anti-synthetase syndrome belongs to the family of idiopathic inflammatory myopathies, which includes dermatomyositis, polymyositis, and immune-mediated necrotising myopathy.

Myositis antibody panel testing is is a crucial investigation in patients with suspected CT-ILD for confirming a diagnosis. It can detect anti-aminoacyl transfer RNA synthetase antibodies, particularly anti-Jo-1, which is present in approximately 80% of patients with anti-synthetase syndrome. Other antibodies in this group include anti-PL-7 and anti-PL-12. A positive result would confirm the diagnosis and guide management.

Anti-nuclear antibody testing is non-specific and may be positive in various autoimmune conditions. ANA is especially associated with systemic lupus erythematosus, a disease not especially associated with interstitial lung disease. While ANA may be positive in anti-synthetase syndrome, it is not the most specific test for confirming the diagnosis.

Muscle biopsy can demonstrate inflammatory changes in myositis but is invasive and unnecessary when serological testing can provide the diagnosis. It would be considered if antibody testing was negative but clinical suspicion remained high.

Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are specific for rheumatoid arthritis and would not help diagnose anti-synthetase syndrome. While patients with anti-synthetase syndrome can have arthritis, it is not typically erosive or associated with these antibodies.

Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage may show non-specific inflammatory changes but would not confirm the diagnosis. It is more helpful in excluding infection or malignancy when these are suspected.

The combination of myositis (elevated creatine kinase and proximal muscle weakness), interstitial lung disease (reduced DLCO and characteristic HRCT changes), mechanic's hands, and constitutional symptoms strongly suggest anti-synthetase syndrome. Early recognition and treatment with immunosuppression are important to prevent the progression of both muscle and lung disease. The condition can overlap with other connective tissue diseases and may be associated with an increased risk of malignancy, similar to dermatomyositis. Treatment typically involves systemic corticosteroids and steroid-sparing agents such as mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide, particularly when significant lung involvement is present.
learningPoints: Anti-synthetase syndrome is characterised by myositis, interstitial lung disease, mechanic's hands, fever, and arthritis
High resolution CT chest in anti-synthetase syndrome typically shows non-specific interstitial pneumonia pattern
Myositis-specific antibody testing is crucial for diagnosis of anti-synthetase syndrome
Anti-synthetase syndrome is classified as an idiopathic inflammatory myopathy, related to dermatomyositis and polymyositis
references: Jee AS, Sheehy R, Hopkins P, Corte TJ, Grainge C, Troy LK, Symons K, Spencer LM, Reynolds PN, Chapman S, De Boer S. Diagnosis and management of connective tissue disease‐associated interstitial lung disease in Australia and New Zealand: a position statement from the Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand. Respirology. 2021 Jan;26(1):23-51. Available at: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/resp.13977
Katzap E, Barilla-LaBarca ML, Marder G. Antisynthetase syndrome. Current rheumatology reports. 2011 Jun;13:175-81. Available at:
